title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text: Trees are tagged with unique identifiers to facilitate consistent, long-term monitoring and data collection critical to urban ecological research. Tagging enables repeated measurements of individual trees, supporting studies on growth rates, health status, phenological changes, carbon sequestration and responses to urban-specific stressors such as urban heat. Tagging also supports spatial analysis and management of the living collections.

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Definitions

Diameter at breast height (DBH)
The diameter of the tree, measured at 1.3 m from the base (the standard height used by the Forestry Commission).

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Installation

Trees with DBH ≥ 5 cm

Attach a unique number tag to the tree at 1.3 m; the nail also serves as a mark for the DBH measurement height.
Nail orientation and placement:

Nail should slant downwards so the tag does not rest against the bark.
Nail only into sapwood.
Always leave at least 1 cm of nail exposed to allow for trunk growth.




Trees with DBH < 5 cm

Consider whether it is useful to tag at this age.
If tagging is appropriate, consider attaching the tag with a loop of wire.
Otherwise, attach the tag with a screw into sapwood (this is more secure than a nail); place at 1.3 m if practical.

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Annual survey
Ideally, conduct the tag survey at the same time as the tree survey. The annual tag check should include:

Check that all suitable trees are tagged.
Check for missing or damaged tags and replace where necessary.
Check that wire loops will not cause girdling in the coming year; replace with nails where appropriate.
If required, back out nails and screws so there is at least 1 cm allowance for growth.
At South Kensington: check that plaques attached to trees to support public programmes are not causing girdling or other damage.

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Appendix I: Numbering scheme

Accommodating existing tree numbering at South Kensington

Numbers 1–27 are reserved to follow the numbering used in Wassells (WAS132/2019). For example, T1 in that report corresponds to tag 0001.
Grouped plantings recorded as a single group (e.g. Silver Birch listed as G1) should be retagged with individual tree numbers when practical.
There are established trees outside the Nature Activity Centre with terminal tags 59 and 63; the trees between these should be retagged using the intermediate numbers.



New tag numbers

Tags 0001–0099 are reserved to cover existing use cases in South Kensington.
In the long term, reserving number blocks for particular geographic areas (for example, Nature Discovery Garden, Tring) will either:

require purchase of a large quantity of tags, or
result in non-sequential blocks of numbers being used.

Tags 100 and above can be applied to any tree on the NHM estate. Geographic localisation of numbers should be handled through the tree database rather than by permanently reserving number blocks.

title: Sensor Network
text: The sensor network project involves deploying environmental monitoring sensors throughout the Nature Discovery Garden to collect real-time data on various environmental parameters.

title: Sensor Network
text: The sensor network project involves deploying environmental monitoring sensors throughout the Nature Discovery Garden to collect real-time data on various environmental parameters.

text:
Objectives

Monitor environmental conditions continuously
Collect data on temperature, humidity, air quality, and other parameters
Provide data for research and public engagement
Support evidence-based garden management decisions

title: Environmental DNA
text: Environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling is used to detect and monitor biodiversity in the Nature Discovery Garden by analyzing genetic material present in environmental samples.

title: Environmental DNA
text: Environmental DNA (eDNA) sampling is used to detect and monitor biodiversity in the Nature Discovery Garden by analyzing genetic material present in environmental samples.

title: Environmental DNA
text:
Objectives

Detect species presence through DNA analysis of soil and water samples
Monitor biodiversity changes over time
Identify cryptic species that may be missed by traditional survey methods
Complement traditional ecological survey techniques

title: MAARU - Autonomous Spatial Audio Recorders
text: MAARU (Autonomous Spatial Audio Recorders) is a collaboration focused on deploying autonomous audio recording devices to monitor soundscapes and wildlife acoustic activity in the Nature Discovery Garden.

title: Case Studies
text:
Environmental monitoring sensors throughout the Nature Discovery Garden collecting real-time data on various environmental parameters.










Using eDNA sampling to detect and monitor biodiversity through genetic material analysis in environmental samples.










Regular moth trapping surveys to monitor lepidopteran diversity and abundance, contributing to long-term biodiversity monitoring.










Standardized bird transect surveys to monitor avian diversity and abundance throughout the year.










Weekly butterfly transect surveys following UK Butterfly Monitoring Scheme protocols to monitor butterfly populations.

title:
Urban Research Stations
at the Natural History Museum
text:
By 2050, nearly 70% of people will live in cities
We study how we can make cities better places for people and for nature.
Our vision is of a future where both people and planet thrive.


30 years of research
In 2025 we will have been studying the Museum’s gardens for thirty years. Regular biodiversity surveys by Museum staff and others make it one of the most intensively studied urban sites worldwide.








A living laboratory
Our gardens are a place where we experiment with new techniques and evaluate new methodologies to better understand how we can restore biodiversity and create resilient ecosystems.
Our gardens and research infrastructures are open to Museum staff and external collaborators for research and teaching.


Training the next generation
We run training, internships and practical projects for students and early-career researchers to build capacity in urban ecology and data-driven conservation.








Integrating diverse datasets
We integrate sensor, observational and specimen data into interoperable datasets to support research, monitoring and evidence-based decision making.


Leading change
We engage with policy-makers, urban planners and community partners to translate research into practical guidance and to promote nature-positive urban change.

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Download PDF
Trees are tagged with unique identifiers to facilitate consistent, long-term monitoring and data collection critical to urban ecological research. Tagging enables repeated measurements of individual trees, supporting studies on growth rates, health status, phenological changes, carbon sequestration and responses to urban-specific stressors such as urban heat. Tagging also supports spatial analysis and management of the living collections.

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Definitions

Diameter at breast height (DBH)
The diameter of the tree, measured at 1.3 m from the base (the standard height used by the Forestry Commission).

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Installation

Trees with DBH ≥ 5 cm

Attach a unique number tag to the tree at 1.3 m; the nail also serves as a mark for the DBH measurement height.
Nail orientation and placement:

Nail should slant downwards so the tag does not rest against the bark.
Nail only into sapwood.
Always leave at least 1 cm of nail exposed to allow for trunk growth.




Trees with DBH < 5 cm

Consider whether it is useful to tag at this age.
If tagging is appropriate, consider attaching the tag with a loop of wire.
Otherwise, attach the tag with a screw into sapwood (this is more secure than a nail); place at 1.3 m if practical.

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Annual survey
Ideally, conduct the tag survey at the same time as the tree survey. The annual tag check should include:

Check that all suitable trees are tagged.
Check for missing or damaged tags and replace where necessary.
Check that wire loops will not cause girdling in the coming year; replace with nails where appropriate.
If required, back out nails and screws so there is at least 1 cm allowance for growth.
At South Kensington: check that plaques attached to trees to support public programmes are not causing girdling or other damage.

title: NHM Urban Research Station Survey Protocol — Tree Tagging
text:
Appendix I: Numbering scheme

Accommodating existing tree numbering at South Kensington

Numbers 1–27 are reserved to follow the numbering used in Wassells (WAS132/2019). For example, T1 in that report corresponds to tag 0001.
Grouped plantings recorded as a single group (e.g. Silver Birch listed as G1) should be retagged with individual tree numbers when practical.
There are established trees outside the Nature Activity Centre with terminal tags 59 and 63; the trees between these should be retagged using the intermediate numbers.



New tag numbers

Tags 0001–0099 are reserved to cover existing use cases in South Kensington.
In the long term, reserving number blocks for particular geographic areas (for example, Nature Discovery Garden, Tring) will either:

require purchase of a large quantity of tags, or
result in non-sequential blocks of numbers being used.

Tags 100 and above can be applied to any tree on the NHM estate. Geographic localisation of numbers should be handled through the tree database rather than by permanently reserving number blocks.
